frontmatter.title: Cross-references
frontmatter.description: Create numbered cross-references to labeled content (e.g. a figure, document, or table) and automatically generates links with hover previews.
frontmatter.subtitle: Link to equations, figures, tables, and so much more!
frontmatter.subject: MyST Markdown

children.value: References refer to labeled content (e.g. a figure, document or table) and automatically generates links and extra information, like numbering. This page covers the basics of setting up references to content and shows examples for sections, figures, tables and equations.
to cite scholarly work and create bibliographies.
There are several ways that you can define cross-references with
Cross-referencing content is accomplished with markdown link syntax (
, like the figure, equation or section header that you are referencing. If you leave the text empty,

will fill in the link with the title, caption, document name, or equation number as appropriate (e.g. “Figure 1” or “Section 1.3.7”). If you do supply text, you can control what is displayed in the reference, as well as have access to placing the name and enumerator of the target, using
Default for numbered references is to fill in the listing and number (e.g. Figure 1.).
Note that headings are numbered on this page, so it will show the number rather than the header title.
Modify the title, but keep the enumerator, you can use
Note that unnumbered targets (e.g. a paragraph) will resolve the number to

Headers resolve the text of the header, if there is a caption in the target it will be used.
For targets that do not have a caption or header, the name will resolve to the label.
If you override the text in the link, that will be used.

Roles are a more explicit way of defining references that provide more configuration at the cost of requiring more complexity to write.
There several roles that define various kinds of references in

.
In many cases, the short-hand syntax described above are aliases for these roles.
Below we describe the most commonly-used ones.
role can be used to bring the title or caption directly in line, the role can take a single argument which is the label, for example,
You can also choose the reference text directly (not taking from the title or caption) by using,

When to use roles vs. short-hand
Short-hand syntax is provided for a quick and clean way to reference content with

if you are happy with default behavior.
However, some roles provide additional configurability that allow you to change their behavior.
Thus, a good rule of thumb is the following:
If you’re happy with the default behavior of references, use
If you only wish to modify the displayed text, use
If you wish to use more complex configurability, use the role syntax (
The content you wish to reference is called a
, and to be targeted that content must have a

.
For example, in this reference syntax:

is the label for the target.
There are many ways that you can label a target, and the sections below describe the most common approaches.
To be referenced, targets must have a
Labels can be given to most directives by setting the
option. For example, to label and reference a figure, use the following syntax:

```{figure} https://github.com/rowanc1/pics/blob/main/mountains.png?raw=true
:label: my-fig
:align: center

My **bold** mountain 🏔🚠.
```

Check out [](#my-fig)!!
You can use this syntax to also reference

```{math}
:label: my-math-label
e=mc^2
```

See [](#my-math-label) for an equation!

children.value: To add labels to a header use
before the header, these can then be used in markdown links and
roles. This is helpful if you want to quickly insert links to other parts of your book. Referencing a heading will show the heading and the subsequent two pieces of content

(my-section)=
#### Header _Targets_

Use `(label)=` before the element that you want to target, then reference content with:

* [](#my-section)
By default headings are not numbered, see
for more information. To turn on numbered headers you need to turn numbering on in the document or project using
in the frontmatter. You can control this for each heading level:
These will show up, for example, as
. To turn on all heading numbering, use
TODO: We should support pandoc style unnumbered {-} and {.class, #id} syntax

By default section numbering for headers is turned off with numbering for figure and table numbering enabled.
To turn on
for headers, you can can change the frontmatter in the document or project. See
for more details on the numbering object.

---
numbering:
  heading_2: true
  heading_3: true
---

(my-chapter)=
## My Chapter

(my-section)=
### My Section

(my-section2)=
### My Second Section

* [](#my-section)

to customize how various components of the page are numbered. By default, numbering is enabled for figures, equations, tables, math, and code blocks; it is disabled for headings and other content types contained on the page. Additionally, by default, numbering resets on every page.
To enable numbering of all content, you may simply use:
object allows you to be much more granular with enabling and disabling specific components:

numbering:
  code: false
  equations: false
  headings: true
You may also add numbering for custom content kinds:

---
numbering:
  box:
    enabled: true
---

:::{figure} image.png
:label: my-box
:kind: box

This figure will be numbered as "Box 1"
:::
By default, all components are numbered sequentially as 1, 2, 3... However, under the
. For now, this applies to all numberings on the page. Instead of enumerating as simply 1, 2, 3... they will follow the template set in
. For example, in Appendix 1, you may want to use the following
so content is enumerated as A1.1, A1.2, A1.3...
If you want to control the numbering for a specific figure, you can use the
option. This will give the figure a specific enumerator, and will not increment the counting for other figures. This is helpful if you want to explicitly count figure
and then carry on counting figures as normal; alternatively you can take control of numbering entirely by setting
You may also redefine how the component will render when referenced in the text by specifying
. For this example, the figures on the page will start with
and when referenced in the text they will appear as “fig (1)”
By default, numbering will reset on every page. However, you may enable continuous numbering across all pages by specifying
object. If this is specified on a single page, it will continue counting from the previous page; if specified in your
, counting will be continuous across the entire project.

value to begin counting on the page at a specific number. This is useful, for example, if you want continuous figure numbering across your project until you reach your appendix, then you want to reset to A1:
By default, page titles are not numbered, even if you turn on all numbering with
Doing so will enumerate all the titles in your
project. These numberings will follow the structure of your table of contents. So, given the structure:

toc:
  - file: index.md
  - file: section_a.md
    children:
      - file: chapter_1.md
      - file: chapter_2.md
  - file: section_b.md
numbering are enabled, these will be incremented together. So, for the example above, if there is a single heading of level 1 in
, it will increment the same count as the title of
You can further customize this behavior by setting an explicit

value on a page. By default the offset matches the nesting level in the table of contents, but, for example, if you want page numbering to be flat, despite nesting in the table of contents, you can set

there is a better chance of your content working in other markdown renderers like GitHub or VSCode.
Note that not everything has a number or name (e.g. a paragraph usually isn’t numbered, and an equation doesn’t have text to resolve into
). An unnumbered node will resolve to

To link to a page, that file must be included in the project’s
. If it isn’t, then the document will be presented as a static file.
The content could be a single paragraph, a figure, table or list. It can also be fully interactive content, with cross-references to other content, allowing you to nest and follow references easily!
